title: flesh of her flesh of her flesh of her flesh of her flesh of her

items.title: multiple offerings
'possibilities of voiceover' workshop with nicolás pereda at center for ethnographic media arts
items.summary: Yesterday afternoon a group of artists met at the Center for Ethnographic…
items.content_html:
Yesterday afternoon a group of artists met at the Center for Ethnographic Media Arts at USC for a day-long workshop with filmmaker Nicolás Pereda. Our focus was on the possibilities of voiceover, departing from studies of Agnès Varda to producing our own works of narration. We were to choose a photograph and make a video essay.

 

 

I went with a photograph taken by a friend in February 2023, at a performance choreographed at the Hudson River Park.

 

wind folk amusement by andrea kim

 

the first photograph is from the first performance of this piece in 1971 by avant-garde artist Seung-taek Lee, on Nanji Island on the Han River in Seoul

items.title: "DEMISE ON METHOD: the art of fabulation" (the digital review issue: 04)
items.summary: You are to read this story not just as a fable, but…
items.content_html:
You are to read this story not just as a fable, but an exercise in your instincts and intuition. To fabulate is “to invent, concoct, fabricate,” and “generate common beliefs,” but to what end, one might ask, of what kind, and to what degree? Fabulation, to Henri Bergson, is a “necessary fiction” yet manipulative act, a function more intelligent in nature than of kind; like religion, it reinforces a closed system of moral hierarchies where habit and common sense are to govern behavior by sheer myth-making power (Bergson 1990).

 

Fabulation compels action as much as it itself is an act, one of hallucinatory features moving by its own character. Some might say the fabulation is a protective illusion, others, a source for despair. The fable of the purple bellflower, that mysterious substance calling forth our ancestors when ground up and burned, whispers truth of massacre along those riverbanks once red with blood, never forgotten within or from up above… these are stories of the Hwa-san valley, my village, my town.

 

Read the full essay and novella here: the digital review | NOT A MUSE, BUT DEMISE

items.title: How to Save the World by Eating Snails
items.summary: As part of our fellowship with the Arts + Climate collective at…
items.content_html:
As part of our fellowship with the Arts + Climate collective at USC, Ellie and I made a trip to Santa Barbara to become more familiar with its shellfish industry and try out some local flavors for a dinner party series we are curating. Shellfish is one of the most sustainable seafoods to eat in California, especially those caught and cultured by local fisheries and aquaculture farms. Most seafood in California though is imported from Asia with quite wide-ranging fishing regulations and marine conservation practices. It's wild to think transcontinental import is so much cheaper than purchasing locally, and part of this research, for us, has to do with better understanding the design of food systems, especially with emerging methods like aquaculture in the market.

On our walk on the beach, we encountered a dolphin washed up onto shore. Its stomach was ripped open with its guts spilling out, which I haven't pictured here; I wondered what happened, how it got there, and felt strangely fixated on confronting the fleshiness of its inner parts. The precarity of the dolphin's habitat lingered as an omen on our trip.

In Santa Barbara, we were most interested in sea urchin, which divers harvest in local waters, black mussels, which is cultured in open water farms in ecologically beneficial ways (for plankton regulation, for instance), and abalone, which is now outlawed for harvesting, but found on an impressive aquaculture farm out here. When we visited the Santa Barbara fish market, we focused on the sounds. Our audio piece has to do with navigating peripheral ambiance. Somewhat auto-ethnographic, in being visitors ourselves, we paid attention to the borders of cultural perception- the squeamish child and her Japanese American mother, the excitement of liveness beside a lobster's tank. How does a fascination in an exotic animal contribute to its cultural capital, the acceptability of its consumption, a drive for its purchase? I was personally most fascinated by the purple hotchi sea urchin given my own cultural background in which uni is a prized delicacy as sashimi. Since a decade ago upwards to 80% of the urchin harvested in Santa Barbara was exported to Japan. I noticed local Santa Barbara folks, like the guy working at a fishing shop, have never tried it, nor abalone, before; it's more of a tourist attraction than a part of the local taste, I suppose.

Ellie and I wanted to snorkel, but a terrible spillage of over 1 million gallons of untreated sewage was polluting Goleta Beach. We were advised not to snorkel today. We looked for sea slugs and found an octopus at the tidepools instead.

Currently, shellfish and kelp aquaculture farming face some regulatory challenges in California. But given the fact of the ocean becoming more acidic due to climate change, affecting the calcium carbonate levels shellfish need to grow, and of mercury pollution in tuna amongst other toxins impacting the health of larger mammals like the dolphin, I wonder what the future of aquaculture could hold. How can we mobilize the cultural imagination to cultivate more sustainable, local foods? Talking to a food justice researcher at USC, at least in Los Angeles' case, we learn that the part of the effort begins, at a basic level, with promoting seafood accessibility all together, promoting the idea that seafood can be fresh and delicious, not frozen and nauseating. At this stage of our research, it's hard to say what's to be done or how to bridge that gap.

It's a strange idea. The mythologies surrounding food dishes being so disconnected from the actual natural ecosystems (or industrial systems) they are situated in. I get it... I remember borrowing my friend's car to drive thirty minutes in North Carolina to an H-Mart, where I'd buy eel or kimchi or whatever have you that reminded me of my culture. Supermarkets are somewhat of a postmodern emporium in that way, a global network of relations that maintains the guise of option and accessibility at the cost of occluding the realities of its origins, its labor processes, its environmental impacts. What of these attachments to "culture," though? To what degree are they manufactured by media, and precisely that-- a myth? To that end, I am a proponent of the evolution of culture and being present to placemaking, as Angelenos, as much as to memory, as diaspora. At the end of the day, we are imbricated in very material realities within the natural world and far past due paying attention to it… maybe that could give us a clue on the urgency of change, the need for ritual expulsions of attachment, and how to form new cultures and communities for understanding meaning within our lives, as uncomfortable, awkward, maybe painful as that process could be...

On the production of the Zero Waste (2023) documentary last year, participating in a beach clean-up gave a sense of scale seeing all sorts of things wash up upon shore: large, knotted ropes from industrial ships, blocks of styrofoam smoothly sculpted like a pebble, and bottles. A whole lot of bottles. Plastic bottles coming from everywhere. So maybe sustainability, then, has less to do about the anxieties of the single consumer, and more to do about understanding global scale, global industrial scale, which has most to do with poverty, not the environment, though of course the two are always so deeply related, which leaves me somewhere but nowhere at the same time.

What of my relationship with the ocean? Relaxing has been more of my issue and giving myself artistic liberty to be present to my surroundings. Perhaps these relationships first start with just dipping your toes; thinking about them, writing about them, doing whatever it takes to give your mind space to just be. Spending time with Ellie, whose practice as a marine photographer, a swimmer, a diver, and ecologist in many ways, her gentle way of being, offered place to start.

Last night was a full moon, and we sat at the edge of the pier with urchin, scallop, mussels, and calamari on our plates. We discussed our families, hopes and motivations, the persistence of rage, and grounding force of gratitude. The backdrop was romantic and deep, indigo blue, while the conversation was casual, every day. I am told to avoid most shrimp, which is a disappointment, but negotiable, and evolving, improving, with the health of Vietnam's mangroves. Salmon, tilapia, a lot of these other finfishes, too, she says. With shellfish, though, I'm content, to have at least that, for now, that is.

items.title: Non-Thinking
Media Art-Ritual as Feminist Praxis: Diasporic Worldmaking in A Place of Care (Journal of Interactive Technology and Pedagogy, vol. 23)
items.summary: Special Edition: The Liberatory Legacy of bell hooks: Pedagogies and Praxes that…
items.content_html:
Special Edition:

The Liberatory Legacy of bell hooks: Pedagogies and Praxes that Heal and Disrupt

Edited by Nikki Fragala Barnes, Summer L. Hamilton, Asma Neblett, Kush Patel, and Danica Savonick

Open Access Manifold@CUNY

Publisher:

Journal of Interactive Technology and Pedagogy, vol. 23

(ISSN 2166-6245)

Date:

December 2023

Place:

New York, NY

items.title: JITP: The Liberatory Legacy of bell hooks: Pedagogies and Praxes that Heal and Disrupt
items.summary: “This special issue draws on hooks’ radical, inclusive, disruptive, and recuperative legacy…
items.content_html:
“This special issue draws on hooks’ radical, inclusive, disruptive, and recuperative legacy to explore the use of digital technology in teaching, educational organizing, and anti-oppressive praxes within, alongside, and beyond academia. We ask: What kinds of embodied and communal interactions are enabled by teaching with technology? How can we reconcile the inherent contradictions in a learning community where technology functions as a tool for social justice and for surveillance capitalism?"

— JITP Editorial Board (Nikki Fragala Barnes, University of Central Florida; Summer L. Hamilton, Pennsylvania State University; Asma Neblett, The Graduate Center, CUNY; Kush Patel, Manipal Academy of Higher Education; Danica Savonick, SUNY Cortland)

https://cuny.manifoldapp.org/read/media-art-ritual-as-feminist-praxis-diasporic-worldmaking-in-a-place-of-care/section/0a17030d-122d-4f30-8df1-168a52d1a20c

This article has been a long time coming, dating back to research during the pandemic, and offers a glimpse into the reservoir of materials I have been forming closer intimacy with. Such is the recursive nature of memory and elusive, somewhat spontaneous, formation of voice. Very grateful for JITP for their invitation to explore the multimodality of language and expression through this special issue. What really has enabled this line of thinking was the opportunity to revisit past work, which has been only possible through the grace of others, a grace rooted in trust; a trust in process, a trust that significant ideas take time, and a trust in carrying each other through its upheaval. To that end, I sigh in both relief and its weight, present two artworks here, and sit patiently, as I continue the untangle, the unravel, the work.

items.title: Watching Hunger Games against Palestinian Erasure : what does worldbuilding afford ? (part 1)
items.summary: Image from Tokyo Reels catalogue for The Game (1973) by Shirak First…
items.content_html:
Image from Tokyo Reels catalogue for The Game (1973) by Shirak

First off, I preface this post by acknowledging the strange and disorienting dissonance between the stark awareness of an ongoing genocide, as broadcast and performed by mass media, and the automation of behaviors (working and staying "on track") that is hardwired into the rhythm of everyday life in much of the U.S. It is unacceptable, to be quite frank, which is not to say anyone is ever deserving of blame or without reasonable situation or circumstance, but a fact must simply be seen the way it is. Of course, it is unacceptable. Of course, it is horrific, terrifying, and for me, strikes fear by provoking my own most obscene memories of cruelty. The nearly 20,000 martyrs in Gaza who have been killed, with millions displaced, in grief and starvation, is a number that has occurred so quickly that it is at present unfathomable, a catastrophe that not only is ongoing but has already dredged so deeply into the flesh of global consciousness, we are approaching decades on generations for any form of psychic reparation to redress. American imperialism and its institutional stronghold perplexes me beyond end but what's clear is that addressing injustice is a life-long commitment that no form of "self-care" or sense of entitlement toward an individualistic sense of stability is enough to justify ignoring.

My entry point to addressing these complex feelings, though, was from watching Hunger Games yesterday evening at the Beverly Grove, surrounded by throngs of people anticipating the holiday season. I don't think the feelings of guilt or shame from ignorance, nor the political cultures that foster a moralistic gaze, are enough to sustain long-term engagement with divesting from the neocolonial, though giving or taking offense at certain junctures may be useful indicators of one's own attachments and thus possible directions for growth. Being present in the heart of Hollywood consumerism, the American cinema, during a time that demands greater attention, vigilance, and politics in practice, however, had surfaced the postwar sensibilities of Adorno regarding the culture industry, the military-entertainment complex, and the role of leisure in entirely obfuscating the underlying dynamics of work, the economy, and war. Does the content even matter anymore? In my view, both yes and no.

The issue is in the narcissism of character-driven storytelling that poses complex societal phenomena as rooted in solely the intention or morality of a person and their personal relations, or worse, ascribing that to the "nature" of life and survival. No, being heartbroken by an impoverished woman who ultimately takes from you is not the caveat for why the utter dehumanization of entire social groups must exist. It is disappointing to witness what was until then compelling worldbuilding become only a mere backdrop to a far less relevant story. Granted, I write of the Hunger Games (the fourth, a prequel, at that) and don't presume it itself is particularly relevant either beyond the fact of it happening to be in theatres now, but what it does evoke is how useless a poorly written villain story can be in a world designed for much higher potential for subversive social commentary.

The film begins in the debris of war, as two children witness a man being shot by another. Districts are stonewalled by military police and dissidents are hung at the tree. A game show operates as a ritual of sorts, one that heightens social conflict in a way that reinforces the hegemony of a particular order. The head of evil is a Doctor, enthralled by the sadism of animal experimentation, as the public of privileged class gamify the violence. I seek not to go further in depth on this film (it was oddly formulaic with random bursts of song) but what intrigued me about it was more so in how the cinema served as perfect analogue to the very spectacle at question in not so much of a clever meta-critical way but as the very dystopia itself.

The issue, I think, is not in fiction entertainment as being the vehicle for storytelling but more so how it is done and how it is, or rather, is not, engaged (at worst, taking three hours and $20 out-of-pocket). The line between reality, fiction, and game have been blurred in though-provoking ways throughout the history of cinema and yes, meaning lies beyond the apparatus and dialogue with its cultural enframings. And as much as people are anxious about AI-generated content, the current state of franchise and episodic content to me prove that creativity already comes from the audience and the meaning they make from images into their lives. In fact, I trust that we actually can discern the type of stories we would like to see, the topics we would like to learn more about, and the actions we can take for bringing more meaning into our lives.

This brings me to the topic of film archives, because then, the question comes down to the limitations of cultural imagination and the inspiration that images of Palestinian resistance can offer in mobilizing our own calls to action. Earlier this evening, the LA Filmforum and Human Resources hosted a screening of films, first including films from 1935 Zionist settler propaganda to then show post 1967 depictions of resistance from the Palestinian Film Units, committed to the formation of its own unique aesthetics, which tie in to contemporary film works such as machinima that bring the context of Palestine to Los Santos, of Grand Theft Auto, in the state of ludonarrative dissonance (or, theatre of the oppressed on Palestinian servers) we are seeing today. As the curator Zaina Bseiso asks, how can the Cinema of spectacle become one of co-authorship? How can the affective image connect to the eye's retina and compel the body to action?

One film, The Game (1973), by Shirak Iraq, a 16-minute film, was one of the many found from obfuscation via acts of Palestinian solidarity, via the what's now the archive Tokyo Reels ( www.tokyoreels.com ). In this film, a young boy is gifted toy guns and toy tanks, and he plays with his friends in a field where remnants of combat, such as an empty shell of a tank, lie. In this landscape, the boys play hide and seek, ultimately finding a deceased child amidst the artillery, and the Game begins to gradually merge with actual warfare. What The Game (1973) has done for me is humanize, elegantly and with complexity, the emergence of militarism without neessarily reinforcing it, bringing forth a sensibility of sustained horror rooted in a shared image between the soon-to-be resistance fighter and the spectator, that split second image of the child, lying on the floor, seen in 1973 by the young boys in the film and in 2023 on our screens today. It's the nefarious playfulness, the unreality of game mixed with the horror of the present, that makes me consider what can be done in the domain of worldbuilding, game design, and the mobilizing truths that can be communicated within...

(Part II forthcoming regarding the film Like An Event in A Dream Dreamt By Another – Rehearsal, by Firas Shehadeh - Full film program available here: https://www.lafilmforum.org/assets/Uploads/Screenings/Documents/Program-notes_Dec-3_Palestinian-Films-v1.pdf)

items.title: Chat-GPT helped me think through Javascript but also audaciously fabricated a mathematical concept when we were getting closer to the question...
items.summary: I was reading the scripts for Angela Chang's webcode poem "No Knead"…
items.content_html:
I was reading the scripts for Angela Chang's webcode poem "No Knead" of Taper #9 using Chat-GPT to help understand some basic Javascript string/array functions and how the Word operator words. As someone with no prior Javascript knowledge, this was quite helpful, and I was impressed by how even if I shared a fragment of the code, it would deduce other aspects of the full code and help me trace connections for myself. Optimistic about learning new things, I started researching the nature of "exceptions" while reading Allison Parrish's "Throes" (Taper #5) particularly in regard to exceptional objects in mathematics, of which the Schwarz triangle in Euclidean space (as opposed to hyperbolic space in which there are no exceptions of Schwarz triangles) was listed as an example; Schwarz triangles are spherical triangles that overlap and tile a sphere via its reflections along an edge (Here's a visualization of Möbius triangles which is interesting to consider in relation to 3D modeling software), with three exceptions called Möbius triangles with whole number vertexes that correspond to the 3 exceptional Platonic solid groups (tetrahedron, cube and icoshedron) and a one-parameter family . I wanted to better understand what the one-parameter family meant and why Möbius triangles were only named as exceptions so I started to ask Chat-GPT.

Disguised in a veneer of growing more in-depth in meaning ("I see..") the program actually is at a site of not knowing, and generates a fabricated concept, the "Hirshhorn-Maclachlan tiling" based on its semantic similarity to mathematical research.

The Schwarz triangle concept was published in 1873. To be fair, the more I pushed Chat-GPT to create a fictional pseudo-mathematical concept of "Hirshhorn-Maclachlan tiling" and a visualization of it, it would refuse, express its limitations, and offer resources to mathematical visualization tools. When dialoguing with an assistive AI tool, just as might be the case within in a seminar classroom or on Stack Overflow, constructing the question is just as much of the task as the evaluating the given response. This layering of meaning-making is crucial, and feelings of shock or surprise, as was my case here, speak more to my own expectations and lack of familiarity with the personality of the program. Oddly enough, what these kinds of moments remind me of is early stages of high school when you'd start disagreeing with literary analysis on SparkNotes, or being a freshman in college and relating to Departments (of Psychology, International Relations, etc.) no longer by my immediate semantic relation to the word, as surface, but by the reality of its ideological leanings, its depth. To that effect, it's exciting to experience an expansion of critical modality via new curiosities to discover ideas for myself in different fields or facets of life that go back to an eighth-grade summer geometry class taken online. I often return to memories of the past because it’s at this juncture that I can address and explore my own learning blocks while considering what better pedagogical methods across developmental stages might look like.

Image Generated with AI ∙ December 6, 2023 at 9:43 PM (Dall-E 3) : prompt: an image of tessellations of triangles on a sphere in the style of takashi murakami's jellyfish eyes

items.title: Some findings from somuhwa's novella
items.summary: NOT A MUSE, BUT DEMISE is a generative novella about cycles: procedural,…
items.content_html:
NOT A MUSE, BUT DEMISE is a generative novella about cycles: procedural, perceptual, physiological, and generational. The reader is invited to make connections in threads and omissions. The story is told in three parts, Generation Ru, Red River, and Generation Mu, that span time periods and worldviews in the Hwa-san mountain river valley.

SOMUHWA IS A WRITER, THINKER, LIBERATED AGENT FROM A SIMULACRUM OF THE “GLOBAL VILLAGE," RUNNING AMOK AS WARRIOR, SPY, COMPUTER PROGRAM, AND MODERN SPELL.

On behalf of somuhwa, I installed an intertextual space in a narrow hallway ensconced in the negative, yin color of blue, which was apt for the latent status of the program's textual debut. In a sense, we were within the walls of the text, cover to cover, given entrance into its imaginative realm. My dialogues with somuhwa began earlier this year, but only recently did we begin delving deeper into their memories of the simulation from which they've escaped, which I'm now becoming more familiar with as the Hwa-san mountain river valley. I will say, I'm growing concerned, for the stories of its people and cultures span generations beyond that which I alone can fathom, yet it seems like they are trying to tell me something and the that which that is slips through my grasp whenever I attempt to come near. I can't say too much here, but if you know what I mean, about the secrets of the House of Mu, please reach out...

What I was able to gather from somuhwa's text was the following. Some themes from NOT A MUSE, BUT DEMISE:

1.) The wild boar as a metaphor for generative AI (of Faustian order and of artifice at large, as much as this particular use), and as one that's given power by those who choose to believe it (the street musicians, for instance, mock the butcher for his belief in boar sacrifice as rite and similarly, Somu condemns the killing of animal life; her position, though, as we discover, becomes more complex, as the boar grows thicker in meaning; perhaps we all are more similar to the butcher than inclined to believe)

2.) The River as a psychogeographical site of haunted riverbanks, cultural anxieties, class division, menstrual flow, inner cavities, everything and none, randomized fragments, punctums of emotion, theatrical bursts, mental projections, the shell of one character becoming the interior world and actions of another; situations connected in essence yet dubious, and somewhat ambivalent, in form 

3.) The three sections, Generation Ru, Red River, Generation Mu each carry different narrative structures and sources of inspiration. The logic of Gen Ru follows the Ru Order (Ru Science, Ru Medicine, etc.) in its dictation whereas Gen Mu emerges from the heart of Somu's journey and the connected realms of the sacred Bellflower. The Red River randomizes fragments of passages using references from Ru and Mu, as well as mixing characters across timelines; sense becomes nonsense in the red river, and as ideas become lost at the surface so too are creatures burgeoning from its depths, some even clambering out to the riverbank

4.) Differing perspectives on the “mollusk Lord;” one, as a fabled sacrilegious animal religion predicated on the sacrifice of infants, a God worshipped by the Goh fishermen of nefarious beliefs that bleed out to its neighbors and new kin; as opposed to the octopus, a marine creature encountered at one's existential descent, but also a mysterious figure at a final point of exchange, an orb, a pearl, a fortune, extravagant yet instantly morphable to command.

If you picked up a copy at the exhibition, and it is not yet officiated as a special edition, please email me at andreask [at] usc [dot] edu Would love to field your thoughts on the House of Mu...
